content.text: Why Licensing Is the Critical Layer

Training data, model weights, generated outputs, and fine-tuned derivatives all travel different legal paths. A single AI deployment can touch four or more distinct licence types simultaneously, and a conflict in any one of them can make the whole pipeline non-compliant. Understanding each layer — and how they interact — is foundational for any organisation building on or with AI systems.

attrs.alt: AI Licensing Landscape — positions common licence types by openness and behavioural restrictions

content.text: The data used to train a model carries its own licence terms, which may restrict downstream uses of the model trained on that data. The key question is whether the training constitutes a sufficiently transformative use to shed the upstream licence conditions.
Disputed — SA may propagate to weights

CC BY-SA's "share-alike" condition theoretically propagates to derivatives. Whether trained model weights are a derivative of the training data is unresolved in every major jurisdiction — but licence risk is real until courts rule.
Auditing Training Data Licences in Practice

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class CommercialStatus(Enum):
    ALLOWED   = "allowed"
    FORBIDDEN = "forbidden"
    DISPUTED  = "disputed"
    UNKNOWN   = "unknown"

@dataclass
class DatasetLicenceProfile:
    dataset_name: str
    licence_spdx: str            # SPDX identifier, e.g. "CC-BY-4.0"
    commercial: CommercialStatus
    copyleft: bool               # SA / GPL-style share-alike
    opt_out_honoured: bool       # True if robots.txt / registry opt-outs removed
    token_count_billions: float

    def risk_flag(self) -> str:
        if self.commercial == CommercialStatus.FORBIDDEN:
            return "CRITICAL: non-commercial data in commercial training"
        if self.copyleft and self.commercial == CommercialStatus.DISPUTED:
            return "HIGH: copyleft data, propagation unresolved"
        if not self.opt_out_honoured:
            return "MEDIUM: opt-out compliance not verified"
        if self.commercial == CommercialStatus.UNKNOWN:
            return "LOW: licence unknown — audit needed"
        return "OK"

# Example audit
datasets = [
    DatasetLicenceProfile("Common Crawl", "custom",  CommercialStatus.ALLOWED,   False, True,  3_500),
    DatasetLicenceProfile("Wikipedia",    "CC-BY-SA-3.0", CommercialStatus.DISPUTED, True,  True,  4),
    DatasetLicenceProfile("Books3",       "unknown",  CommercialStatus.UNKNOWN,   False, False, 37),
    DatasetLicenceProfile("PubMed",       "PMC-OA",   CommercialStatus.ALLOWED,   False, True,  14),
]

for d in datasets:
    print(f"{d.dataset_name:20s}  {d.risk_flag()}")

# Common Crawl        OK
# Wikipedia           HIGH: copyleft data, propagation unresolved
# Books3              LOW: licence unknown — audit needed
# PubMed              OK
When a model is released publicly, the weights are distributed under a licence that governs how the weights may be used, modified, and redistributed. Three families dominate:

Apache 2.0 and MIT are the most common for open-weight models. They allow commercial use, modification, and redistribution with minimal obligations (attribution and, for Apache 2.0, retention of the NOTICE file). They impose
— you may fine-tune the model for any purpose the law allows.
— attribution, retain NOTICE, patent grant, no trademark use. Used by: Mistral 7B (original), MPT models.
— attribution only. Minimal overhead. Less common for large models.
Many frontier models are released with a custom licence that prohibits certain uses regardless of whether the law would allow them:
API ToS only — weights not released
No reverse engineering; output-use restrictions per ToS
No use for model training without permission
Monthly active users >700M requires separate agreement; no use to train competing LLMs
Permissive — unusual for a frontier model

to an otherwise permissive base. The annex lists prohibited uses (generating CSAM, weapons of mass destruction, voter suppression, etc.) and propagates with the model — any downstream redistribution must carry the same annex.
OpenRAIL (BigScience) applies this to open weights. CreativeML OpenRAIL-M governs Stable Diffusion and many image models. Key differences from Apache 2.0:
Use restrictions survive redistribution — you cannot grant your users freedoms the annex forbids.
Non-OSI-approved — cannot be bundled in OSI-conformant open source projects without review.
The licence on trained model weights says nothing about what licence the model's
(Addressed in the Output Ownership sub-subtopic.) If no copyright subsists, the output is in the public domain by default.
What does the platform ToS say?
API providers typically assert a broad licence for outputs to allow them to provide the service, but grant usage rights back to you.
"You retain all rights to Input. We assign to you all our rights, title, and interest, if any, in and to Output." — OpenAI API Terms (2024)

This assignment clause transfers whatever rights the provider might hold — but if the output has no copyright (pure AI generation without human expression), there are no rights to assign, and the clause is hollow. The practical result is public-domain-equivalent outputs that anyone can copy freely.
Layer 4 — Fine-Tuning and Derivative Model Licences
When you fine-tune a base model, the resulting weights are a derivative of the base. The base model's licence governs what you may do with that derivative:

Copyleft 'viral' effect: if you distribute fine-tuned weights trained on GPL-licensed code, you may be obligated to release those weights under GPL. This is contested but legally plausible — seek legal advice before doing so.
The following Python utility checks whether a stack of licences is jointly compatible for a specific deployment scenario:

from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass

class LicenceFamily(Enum):
    PERMISSIVE   = "permissive"     # MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD
    COPYLEFT     = "copyleft"       # GPL, AGPL, CC-BY-SA
    RAIL         = "rail"           # OpenRAIL, RAIL-M
    PROPRIETARY  = "proprietary"    # custom commercial
    CC_NC        = "cc_nc"          # any CC BY-NC variant
    PUBLIC_DOMAIN = "public_domain" # CC0

@dataclass
class LicenceLayer:
    name: str
    layer: str    # "training_data" | "base_model" | "fine_tune" | "output"
    family: LicenceFamily
    commercial_allowed: bool
    copyleft: bool
    propagates_restrictions: bool   # RAIL-style annex or copyleft

class CompatibilityChecker:
    def check(self, layers: list[LicenceLayer]) -> dict:
        issues = []

        # Commercial check
        if any(not L.commercial_allowed for L in layers):
            bad = [L.name for L in layers if not L.commercial_allowed]
            issues.append(f"NON_COMMERCIAL: {bad} forbid commercial use")

        # Copyleft propagation
        copyleft = [L for L in layers if L.copyleft and L.propagates_restrictions]
        if copyleft:
            issues.append(
                f"COPYLEFT_PROPAGATION: {[L.name for L in copyleft]} "
                "may require releasing derivative weights under the same licence"
            )

        # RAIL annex stacking
        rails = [L for L in layers if L.family == LicenceFamily.RAIL and L.propagates_restrictions]
        if len(rails) > 1:
            issues.append(
                f"RAIL_STACKING: multiple RAIL annexes ({[r.name for r in rails]}) "
                "— ensure restrictions are compatible"
            )

        return {
            "compatible": len(issues) == 0,
            "issues": issues,
            "recommendation": "CLEARED for deployment" if not issues else "LEGAL REVIEW REQUIRED",
        }

checker = CompatibilityChecker()

# Example: fine-tuning Llama 3 (8B) on CC BY-NC data for a commercial product
stack = [
    LicenceLayer("Common Crawl", "training_data", LicenceFamily.PERMISSIVE, True, False, False),
    LicenceLayer("CC BY-NC Books", "training_data", LicenceFamily.CC_NC, False, False, False),
    LicenceLayer("Llama 3 Community", "base_model", LicenceFamily.PROPRIETARY, True, False, True),
    LicenceLayer("OpenRAIL-M", "fine_tune", LicenceFamily.RAIL, True, False, True),
]

result = checker.check(stack)
print(result["recommendation"])
for issue in result["issues"]:
    print(" !", issue)

# LEGAL REVIEW REQUIRED
#  ! NON_COMMERCIAL: ['CC BY-NC Books'] forbid commercial use
The checker surfaces the blocking issue: CC BY-NC training data cannot be used in a commercial pipeline. Swap those datasets for CC BY or CC0 equivalents to clear it.

"Open-weight" means the model parameters are publicly downloadable. "Open-source" has a precise OSI definition: the software must allow free redistribution, source code, derivative works, and non-discrimination. Most popular AI models are
Llama 3 prohibits use for competing LLMs and has user thresholds — not OSI-conformant.
Stable Diffusion (CreativeML OpenRAIL-M) has use restrictions — not OSI-conformant.
Mistral 7B under Apache 2.0 is OSI-conformant open source.
Falcon 180B under TII Falcon Licence — custom, not OSI-conformant.
The distinction matters for organisations with open-source policies, procurement rules, or legal departments that treat OSI-approved licences differently from custom terms.
Before shipping any AI product, walk each layer of the stack:

AI Licensing Checklist
======================

1. TRAINING DATA
   [ ] Identify all datasets; obtain SPDX licence identifiers
   [ ] Flag any CC BY-NC, CC BY-SA, or GPL-licensed components
   [ ] Confirm opt-out honoured (robots.txt, TDM registry)
   [ ] Document token counts per licence category

2. BASE MODEL
   [ ] Read full licence text (not just marketing summary)
   [ ] Identify commercial use restrictions and user thresholds
   [ ] Note RAIL / use-restriction annex if present
   [ ] Confirm fine-tuning rights

3. FINE-TUNED WEIGHTS
   [ ] Confirm derived model licence is compatible with deployment intent
   [ ] If distributing externally: propagating restrictions (RAIL, copyleft)?
   [ ] Register model card with licence information

4. OUTPUTS
   [ ] What does the platform ToS grant / restrict?
   [ ] Does copyright subsist (human authorship contribution)?
   [ ] Are outputs subject to RAIL annex use restrictions?
   [ ] Attribution requirements for generated content?

5. DEPLOYMENT
   [ ] Indemnification programme enrolled (if available)?
   [ ] Legal review for RAIL stacking or copyleft propagation?
   [ ] Compliance monitoring for licence version updates?

AI licensing operates across four independent layers — training data, model weights, fine-tuned derivatives, and generated outputs — each with distinct obligations. OSI-permissive licences (Apache 2.0, MIT) impose minimal constraints but provide no behavioural guardrails; proprietary licences restrict usage but provide service SLAs; RAIL/OpenRAIL licences open the weights while banning specific harmful uses. Copyleft risk from CC BY-SA training data and non-commercial restrictions from CC BY-NC are the two most common blockers in commercial AI pipelines. A systematic per-layer audit — formalised in a
and a compliance checklist — is the minimum viable governance process for any team building on third-party models or data.
